summary:
The early Elamite civilization, centered on the lowland region of Khuzestan (ancient Susiana) in what is now southwestern Iran, emerged as a complex polity during the fourth millennium BCE, developing contemporaneously with and partly in response to the rise of Sumerian urbanism in neighboring Mesopotamia. Elam represents one of the world's earliest independent literate civilizations, and its interactions with Mesopotamia — alternating between trade, competition, and military conflict — formed a crucial dynamic of Near Eastern history for over two thousand years.

The heartland of early Elam was the city of Susa, strategically located on the Karun River at the foot of the Zagros mountains, at the junction of lowland Khuzestan and the upland Iranian plateau. Susa had been a major settlement since at least 4000 BCE and was a participant in the [[event:1622|Uruk Expansion]] network, adopting elements of Mesopotamian administrative practice including proto-cuneiform style record-keeping. However, the Elamites did not simply copy Mesopotamian models: they developed their own distinctive administrative system, the [[event:1645|Proto-Elamite script]], which represents an independent writing tradition of considerable geographic reach.

Early Elamite society was organized around palatial and temple institutions comparable in function to those of Mesopotamia, managing agricultural surpluses and craft production in a redistributive economy. Archaeological evidence from Susa and related sites reveals sophisticated metalworking, ceramic production, and evidence for long-distance trade connections spanning the Iranian plateau from Baluchistan to the Caspian. The Elamites controlled important trade routes connecting Mesopotamia to the metal, stone, and luxury resources of the Iranian highlands and beyond, giving them economic leverage that underpinned their political independence.

Elam would go on to become one of the most persistent civilizations of the ancient Near East, surviving — despite periods of Mesopotamian dominance — until its conquest by the Achaemenid Persians in the sixth century BCE. The Elamite language, which has no known relatives and remained in use for over three thousand years, is one of the most distinctive linguistic traditions of the ancient world. The early formation of Elamite civilization during the fourth millennium BCE established the foundations for this long and remarkable history.
